articles.title: Unlocking Real-time Predictions with Shopify's Machine Learning Platform - Shopify
ShopifyQL Notebooks: Simplifying Querying with Commerce Data Models - Shopify
How to Export Datadog Metrics for Exploration in Jupyter Notebooks - Shopify
Double Entry Transition Tables: How We Track State Changes At Shopify - Shopify
ParaRNN: Large-Scale Nonlinear RNNs, Trainable in Parallel

nodes.name: Replication
nodes.definition: Replication is the process of creating and maintaining multiple copies of data or system components. This is often done to improve availability, fault tolerance, and read performance. If one copy fails, others can continue to serve requests, ensuring the system remains operational.

nodes.name: Large Language Models (LLMs)
nodes.definition: AI models trained on vast amounts of text data, capable of understanding and generating human-like language. The ParaRNN framework aims to make training large RNNs competitive for LLM applications.

evidence_quotes.text: enabling large-scale training for the first time and widening the set of architecture choices available to practitioners in designing LLMs, particularly for resource-constrained deployment.

nodes.name: Merlin
nodes.definition: Shopify's machine learning platform designed to support varying requirements for machine learning workflows, including real-time predictions.

evidence_quotes.text: In 2022, we shipped Merlin, Shopify’s new and improved machine learning platform built on Ray.

nodes.name: Ray
nodes.definition: A framework used as the foundation for Shopify's Merlin machine learning platform, supporting its flexible architecture.

evidence_quotes.text: built on Ray

nodes.name: Merlin Online Inference
nodes.definition: A component of the Merlin platform enabling the deployment and serving of machine learning models for real-time predictions at scale.

evidence_quotes.text: Merlin Online Inference, which provides the ability to deploy and serve machine learning models for real-time predictions.

nodes.name: Comet ML
nodes.definition: A tool used within the Merlin platform for managing model registries and tracking machine learning experiments.

evidence_quotes.text: Model Registry and experiment tracking with Comet ML.

nodes.name: Pano Feature Store
nodes.definition: An offline and online feature store developed at Shopify, built upon the Feast open-source feature store.

evidence_quotes.text: Pano Feature Store, an offline / online feature store built on top of an open source feature store, Feast.

nodes.name: Feast
nodes.definition: An open-source feature store that serves as the foundation for Shopify's Pano Feature Store, enabling efficient feature management.

evidence_quotes.text: built on top of an open source feature store, Feast.

nodes.name: Online Inference
nodes.definition: A machine learning pattern where predictions are computed in real-time as new data becomes available, contrasting with batch inference.

evidence_quotes.text: online inference is the ability to compute predictions in real-time as the input becomes available.

nodes.name: Batch Inference
nodes.definition: A machine learning pattern where predictions are computed periodically on a defined set of data, typically used for non-real-time analytical tasks.

evidence_quotes.text: While batch inference can run periodically on a finite set of data, online inference is the ability to compute predictions in real-time as the input becomes available.

nodes.name: Kubernetes
nodes.definition: An open-source container orchestration system used by Shopify to deploy and manage services, including Merlin Online Inference instances.

evidence_quotes.text: These services are deployed on Shopify’s Kubernetes clusters (Google Kubernetes Engine) as any other Shopify service.

nodes.name: Docker
nodes.definition: A platform used for developing, shipping, and running applications in containers, utilized here to package the serving layer for Merlin Online Inference services.

evidence_quotes.text: The serving layer is added to the Merlin Docker image, which is created by Podman from a dedicated Dockerfile.

nodes.name: Autoscaling
nodes.definition: A system behavior where resources automatically adjust to meet demand, ensuring services can handle varying loads efficiently.

evidence_quotes.text: allow online inference services to scale to Shopify’s capabilities.

nodes.name: Data Modeling
nodes.definition: The process of creating a conceptual representation of data, defining its structure, relationships, and constraints. This article details a specific data model for tracking temporal state changes.

evidence_quotes.text: In building commerce data models our main focus was allowing business-savvy users to query data in an intuitive way without any SQL expertise.

We created individual attribute tables for both the Shopify Balance account status and the Shopify account status. An attribute input table must have a specific set of columns: - a partition key that’s common across attribute, which in our case is an account_id - a sort key, generally a transition_at timestamp and an index - an attribute you want to track.

nodes.name: ShopifyQL
nodes.definition: A proprietary query language developed by Shopify, designed to simplify data exploration and analysis for business users within the commerce domain.

evidence_quotes.text: ShopifyQL Notebooks is powered by its very own query language ShopifyQL, and by data models built specifically for commerce.

nodes.name: Granularity
nodes.definition: The level of detail or specificity in a data model, referring to the time grain (e.g., second, month) or the unit each row represents, which impacts the ability to answer specific business questions.

evidence_quotes.text: Finally, we had to determine proper granularity for our data model—in other words, what does each row in our data model represent?

nodes.name: Additive Metrics
nodes.definition: Metrics that can be summed across different dimensions without double-counting or producing illogical results, simplifying data aggregation and analysis for users.

evidence_quotes.text: Providing users with fully additive metrics across all dimensions can make exploring datasets easier.

nodes.name: Dimensions
nodes.definition: Attributes or categories within a data model that allow users to slice, dice, and drill down into data to gain insights, such as 'sales channel' or 'product title'.

evidence_quotes.text: For example, we provided merchants with dimensions like “sales channel” and “product title” so they can drill down on things that matter most to them.

nodes.name: Data Stories
nodes.definition: A method of communicating data insights and findings in a narrative format, often incorporating visualizations and explanations, to help users understand business metric changes.

evidence_quotes.text: which enables merchants to explore how their business metrics are changing, understand the reasons for the change, and share their findings as rich data stories.

nodes.name: Data Quality
nodes.definition: The measure of the condition of data, ensuring it is fit for its intended use, which involves evaluating its accuracy, completeness, and consistency.

evidence_quotes.text: Assess Data Quality and Consistency

nodes.name: Data Consistency
nodes.definition: Ensuring that data values are uniform and do not contradict each other across different datasets or within the same dataset, crucial for reliable analysis.

evidence_quotes.text: Assess Data Quality and Consistency

nodes.name: Querying
nodes.definition: The process of retrieving specific information from a data store or data model based on defined criteria, used here to explore business metrics.

evidence_quotes.text: enables merchants to explore how their business metrics are changing, understand the reasons for the change

nodes.name: BigQuery
nodes.definition: A fully managed, serverless data warehouse that enables super-fast SQL queries using the processing power of Google's infrastructure.

evidence_quotes.text: After that, you can easily load it into the data platform of your choice such as BigQuery or Trino.

nodes.name: Trino
nodes.definition: A distributed SQL query engine designed for running interactive analytic queries against data sources of all sizes, from gigabytes to petabytes.

evidence_quotes.text: After that, you can easily load it into the data platform of your choice such as BigQuery or Trino.

nodes.name: Datadog
nodes.definition: A monitoring and analytics platform used for collecting and visualizing metrics from various sources.

evidence_quotes.text: Datadog has a limited set of visualizations that can be built and it doesn't have the tooling to perform more complex analysis

nodes.name: REST API
nodes.definition: A representational state transfer architectural style commonly used for building web services, providing an endpoint for clients to request predictions from machine learning models.

evidence_quotes.text: Datadog has a REST API which we’ll use to extract data from.
Exposes an endpoint for the inference function that will take features as inputs and return predictions

nodes.name: Jupyter Notebooks
nodes.definition: An open-source web application that allows users to create and share documents containing live code, equations, visualizations, and narrative text, commonly used for data analysis and scientific computing.

evidence_quotes.text: For the purposes of this blog, we’ll be running our analysis in Jupyter notebooks.

nodes.name: API Keys
nodes.definition: Secret tokens used to authenticate requests to an API, granting access to specific resources or functionalities.

evidence_quotes.text: You’ll need credentials (an API key and an APP key) to interact with the datadog API.

nodes.name: Metrics
nodes.definition: Quantifiable measurements used to track and assess the status, performance, or health of a system or process.

evidence_quotes.text: Is there a way to extract Datadog metrics in Python for in-depth analysis?

nodes.name: Time-series data
nodes.definition: A sequence of data points indexed in time order, often used to track changes and trends over time.

evidence_quotes.text: Specify the parameters for time-series data extraction.

nodes.name: API Rate Limiting
nodes.definition: A mechanism used to control the number of requests a client can make to an API within a specific time period, preventing abuse and ensuring service availability.

evidence_quotes.text: One thing to keep in mind is that Datadog has a rate limit of API requests.

nodes.name: Data Granularity
nodes.definition: The level of detail or precision in a dataset. Higher granularity means more detailed data, while lower granularity means more aggregated or summarized data.

evidence_quotes.text: Datadog dashboards have a fixed width for the visualizations, which means that checking metrics across a larger time frame will make the metric data less granular.

nodes.name: Dataframe
nodes.definition: A two-dimensional, size-mutable, potentially heterogeneous tabular data structure with labeled axes (rows and columns), commonly used in data analysis libraries like Pandas.

evidence_quotes.text: Lastly, convert the lists to a dataframe and return it:

nodes.name: Data Visualization
nodes.definition: The graphical representation of data to help understand trends, outliers, and patterns within the data.

evidence_quotes.text: Now, we can use this to visualize data using any tool we want.

nodes.name: Double Entry Transition Tables
nodes.definition: A data presentation format that tracks changes in attributes of entities over time by outputting two rows for each state change, including a 'net_change' column, which simplifies queries and reduces maintenance.

evidence_quotes.text: Enter double entry transition tables.

nodes.name: Net Change Column
nodes.definition: A column in double entry transition tables that indicates the direction of a state change, typically +1 for a new state and -1 for a previous state. Its additive nature allows for easy aggregation of current states.

evidence_quotes.text: net_change column, but it essentially works like this: if you want to track the status of something over time, every time the status changes from one state to another or vice versa, there will be two entries: - net_change = -1: this row is the previous state - net_change = +1: this row is the new state

nodes.name: Data Pipeline
nodes.definition: A series of data processing steps designed to ingest, transform, and load data. In this context, it involves preparing input tables and generating the double entry transition table.

evidence_quotes.text: In this post, we’ll explore how we built a data pipeline using double entry transition tables to answer our question: how many Shopify merchants are using Shopify Balance?

nodes.name: PySpark
nodes.definition: A Python API for Apache Spark, used here for data transformation tasks, such as converting standard transition tables into attribute tables and creating the final double entry transition table.

evidence_quotes.text: Using a standard transition table, we can convert it to an attribute with a simple PySpark job:

nodes.name: Attribute Tables
nodes.definition: Intermediate tables that store specific attributes of entities over time, serving as inputs for constructing more complex transition tables like the double entry transition table.

evidence_quotes.text: First, we need to prepare individual attribute tables to be used as inputs for our double entry transition data infrastructure.

nodes.name: Partition Key
nodes.definition: A key used to distribute data across different partitions or nodes in a distributed system. In this context, 'account_id' serves as the partition key for grouping related data.

evidence_quotes.text: a partition key that’s common across attribute, which in our case is an account_id

nodes.name: Sort Key
nodes.definition: A key used to order data within a partition. Here, 'transition_at' timestamp and 'index' are used to ensure chronological ordering and handle ties for events.

evidence_quotes.text: a sort key, generally a transition_at timestamp and an index

nodes.name: State Changes
nodes.definition: The modification of an entity's attributes from one value to another over time. Tracking these changes is the primary goal of the double entry transition tables.

evidence_quotes.text: track the state changes of both accounts simultaneously

nodes.name: SQL
nodes.definition: Structured Query Language, a standard language for managing and querying relational databases. It is used here for querying the generated transition tables.

evidence_quotes.text: simplifying our queries and reducing long term maintenance on our reporting.

nodes.name: ETL
nodes.definition: Extract, Transform, Load. A process for moving data from source systems, transforming it into a usable format, and loading it into a destination system. The described pipeline follows this pattern.

evidence_quotes.text: we need to prepare individual attribute tables to be used as inputs for our double entry transition data infrastructure. ... This system (called build merge state transitions) takes our individual attribute tables and first generates a combined set of unique rows... It then creates one column per attribute... This table is then run through another process that creates our net_change column and assigns a +1 value to all current rows. It also inserts a second row for each state change with a net_change value of -1.

nodes.name: Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs)
nodes.definition: A type of neural network where connections between nodes form a directed graph along a temporal sequence, allowing them to exhibit temporal dynamic behavior. Their sequential computation is efficient at inference but historically difficult to parallelize during training.

evidence_quotes.text: Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) are naturally suited to efficient inference, requiring far less memory and compute than attention-based architectures, but the sequential nature of their computation has historically made it impractical to scale up RNNs to billions of parameters.

nodes.name: Parallel Training
nodes.definition: A method for training machine learning models by distributing the computational workload across multiple processing units or machines. This is crucial for scaling models to large sizes and reducing training time.

evidence_quotes.text: Apple researchers share a new framework for parallelized RNN training that achieves a 665× speedup over the traditional sequential approach

nodes.name: Linearity
nodes.definition: A property of mathematical operations where scaling input by a factor scales output by the same factor, and the sum of scaled inputs corresponds to the scaled sum of outputs. In RNNs, linearity enables parallelization due to its associative property.

evidence_quotes.text: Modern recurrent architectures have leveraged a clever workaround to enable sequence parallelization: simplifying the recurrence relationship to be purely linear in the hidden state.

nodes.name: Parallel Reduction
nodes.definition: An algorithm that combines elements of a collection into a single result using parallel processing. It's essential for efficiently computing operations like sums or products across long sequences in parallel.

evidence_quotes.text: This mathematical property allows us to use parallel reduction algorithms (also known as parallel scan) to compute the entire sequence of hidden states simultaneously.

nodes.name: Newton's Method
nodes.definition: An iterative numerical method for finding successively better approximations to the roots (or zeroes) of a real-valued function. In this context, it's adapted to solve nonlinear RNN systems by iteratively approximating them with linear systems.

evidence_quotes.text: The key insight is that we can have the best of both worlds, by adapting Newton’s method — a classic numerical technique for solving nonlinear equations.

nodes.name: Jacobians
nodes.definition: A matrix of all first-order partial derivatives of a vector-valued function. In ParaRNN, Jacobians are used to linearize the nonlinear RNN system, allowing it to be solved using methods similar to linear SSMs.

evidence_quotes.text: Newton’s method solves this system iteratively, approximating the nonlinearities with a linearization given by their local derivatives (i.e., their Jacobians).

nodes.name: GRU (Gated Recurrent Unit)
nodes.definition: A type of recurrent neural network that uses gating mechanisms to control information flow, simplifying the architecture compared to LSTMs while maintaining strong performance. ParaRNN adapts GRU cells for parallel training.

evidence_quotes.text: In our experiments, we apply this methodology to adaptations of two classical RNNs: the GRU and LSTM cells

nodes.name: LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory)
nodes.definition: A type of recurrent neural network capable of learning long-term dependencies, utilizing gating mechanisms (input, forget, output gates) and a cell state. ParaRNN adapts LSTM cells for parallel training.

evidence_quotes.text: In our experiments, we apply this methodology to adaptations of two classical RNNs: the GRU and LSTM cells

nodes.name: CUDA Kernels
nodes.definition: Custom programs written in CUDA C/C++ that run on NVIDIA GPUs. They are used here to accelerate the parallel reduction of Jacobian matrices, optimizing performance for large-scale computations.

evidence_quotes.text: To get the best speedups, we then implement custom CUDA kernels to perform efficient parallel reduction of Jacobians presenting these structures.

nodes.name: PyTorch
nodes.definition: An open-source machine learning framework developed by Facebook's AI Research lab. It provides flexibility and speed for building and training neural networks, and is used here for implementing ParaRNN cells.

evidence_quotes.text: In Figure 7 we provide timing results for the three implementations we provide to apply our ParaRNN cells in parallel: in pure PyTorch, in PyTorch with CUDA-accelerated reduction, and fully-fused in CUDA.

nodes.name: Selective State Space Models (SSMs)
nodes.definition: A class of models that combine aspects of state-space models and recurrent neural networks, designed for efficient sequence modeling. They often employ linear recurrence relations that are amenable to parallelization.

evidence_quotes.text: Selective state space models (SSMs) like Mamba use a recurrence in the form:

nodes.name: Mamba
nodes.definition: A specific type of selective state space model known for its efficiency and performance in sequence modeling tasks. It serves as inspiration and a point of comparison for the ParaRNN framework.

evidence_quotes.text: Selective state space models (SSMs) like Mamba use a recurrence in the form:

nodes.name: Transformers
nodes.definition: A deep learning model architecture relying on self-attention mechanisms, widely used in NLP. They process sequences in parallel but have quadratic computational cost with sequence length, unlike RNNs' constant inference cost.

evidence_quotes.text: whereas the computation required for a single forward pass through an RNN is the same regardless of how much context came before. This enables constant-time token generation during inference, making them particularly attractive for efficient deployment.

nodes.name: Inference
nodes.definition: The process of using a trained machine learning model to make predictions on new, unseen data. RNNs are noted for their efficiency during inference due to their constant-time token generation.

evidence_quotes.text: Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) are naturally suited to efficient inference, requiring far less memory and compute than attention-based architectures

nodes.name: Low Latency
nodes.definition: A critical design consideration for online inference systems, aiming to minimize the time delay between receiving a request and returning a prediction.

evidence_quotes.text: Low latency, in order to optimize processing of an inference request to provide responses with minimal delay.

nodes.name: Observability
nodes.definition: The ability to understand the internal state of a system by examining its outputs, crucial for monitoring and debugging deployed services.

evidence_quotes.text: State-of-the-art features for online inference services, like rolling deployments, autoscaling, observability, automatic model updates, etc.

nodes.name: Rolling Deployments
nodes.definition: A deployment strategy that gradually updates instances of a service, minimizing downtime and risk during software updates.

evidence_quotes.text: State-of-the-art features for online inference services, like rolling deployments, autoscaling, observability, automatic model updates, etc.
